cataloging_subject.label: Emaré
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English tail-rhyme romance, not Cotton MS Caligula A II as a physical miscellany, Rickert's 1907 scholarly edition, its prior or later editions, the U-M digital encoding, the repository XML artifact, or modern translations and adaptations.

catalog_summary.title: Emaré
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth century; uniquely attested in a manuscript copied 1446-1460
catalog_summary.region: Composition place uncertain: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia; the unique copy is assigned to the South-East or South-East Midlands

assertion.notes: The manuscript explicit spells the name Emare; the acute accent is established modern title practice.

titles.value: Ihesu, þat ys kyng in trone, As þou shoope boþe sonne and mone
notes: Expansion letters follow Rickert's edited reading; punctuation is editorial.

assertion.notes: No author name is supplied by the unique witness or accepted by the scholarly sources used here.
DIME gives the late fourteenth century; the range is not normalized to exact years because the cited sources do not supply them.

notes: Do not replace this work-level estimate with Rickert's 1907 publication date or the unique witness's fifteenth-century copying date.

assertion.notes: The current British Library component record gives the broader second half of the fifteenth century; DIME and Rickert support the narrower 1446-1460 range.

places.label: Northeast Midlands or East Anglia, England
notes: DIME presents these as uncertain alternatives for composition; this combined place record does not claim a point location.

assertion.notes: DIME says the place of composition is uncertain; Laskaya and Salisbury describe late-fourteenth-century Northeast Midlands or East Anglian dialect features.

notes: The unique witness's South-East or South-East Midlands origin is a different, physical-copy layer recorded in lineage.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML's coarse TEXT/@LANG='eng' is not treated as evidence that the medieval poem is modern English.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas, normally described by DIME as aa4b3cc4b3dd4b3ee4b3, with metrical and rhyme-scheme variations.

assertion.notes: The closing stanza calls the story one of the lays of Britain; modern scholarship catalogs it among the Middle English Breton lays.

subject_statements.label: Exile, sea voyages, separation, and family reunion

summaries.value: Emaré, daughter of Emperor Artyus, refuses her father's attempt to marry her and is set adrift wearing a marvelous gemmed and embroidered robe. Rescued in Galys, she marries its king, but his mother intercepts and forges letters after Emaré bears Segramour, causing mother and child to be cast out at sea again. They reach Rome, where Emaré supports them through needlework. Her husband and later her repentant father come to Rome on pilgrimage or penance; Segramour helps reveal her identity, and the separated family is reconciled.

The poem survives uniquely in Cotton MS Caligula A II, ff. 71r-76v, among many religious, didactic, medical, historical, and romance texts. CME00009 does not represent the complete manuscript or complete Rickert dissertation: it encodes the dissertation title page and the poem on printed pp. 1-32, including Rickert's marginal summaries and page footnotes.

extent.description: The XML poem contains 86 numbered LG stanza groups and 1,035 direct L elements, matching DIME's 1,035-line description of the unique witness. It also contains 32 printed-page milestones, 345 line-number milestones, 12 manuscript-leaf milestones, 105 editorial footnotes, and 283 marginal prose summaries. Those notes and milestones describe Rickert's edition and the encoding, not additional intrinsic poem parts.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00009.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00009; preserves Rickert's title page, complete edited poem, 86 stanza groups, 1,035 lines, current manuscript foliation 71r-76v, marginal summaries, footnotes, italic expansions, and the explicit.

Counts 86 LG, 1,035 L, 32 body PB, 345 line-number MILESTONE, 12 leaf MILESTONE, 105 footnotes, 283 marginal notes, 1,147 HI, 36 ABBR, and one SUP element. The leaf milestones run from 71 through 76 verso.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00009.
evidence.locator: item information, publication, contents, rights, and full text
evidence.summary: Identifies Rickert's title, Chicago publication by R. Clay & Sons in 1907, and the title-page and Emare content units exposed by the digital text.

evidence.citation: Edith Rickert, ed., The Romance of Emaré: Re-edited from the MS with Introduction, Notes, and Glossary (Chicago: Richard Clay & Sons, Limited, 1907).
evidence.locator: title page; preface; introduction pp. ix-l; text pp. 1-32; notes pp. 33-48; glossary pp. 49-54; index p. 55
evidence.summary: Names the work and editor, identifies Cotton Caligula A II and its current and legacy foliation, prints the 1,035-line poem, dates and discusses the manuscript and work, explains editorial practice, and documents earlier editions and proposed sources.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A II, ff. 3-139, record 041-001102312.
evidence.locator: component title/contents, date, origin, custody, and access
evidence.summary: The exact institutional component record identifies Emare (Boffey 1766) on ff. 71r-76v, dates the first manuscript unit to the second half of the fifteenth century, assigns it to England, and lists its surrounding contents.

evidence.locator: date, place, form, IMEV number, keywords, plot, manuscript, and editions
evidence.summary: Dates composition to the late fourteenth century, leaves its place uncertain between the Northeast Midlands and East Anglia, defines the twelve-line tail-rhyme stanza, assigns IMEV 1766, identifies the unique 1,035-line witness on ff. 71r-76v, and supplies a scholarly plot summary and edition history.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'London, British Library, MS Cotton Caligula A.ii.'
evidence.locator: date, place, material, dimensions, codicology, digitization note, and contents
evidence.summary: Dates the first unit 1446-1460, assigns it to the South-East or South-East Midlands, explains the two formerly independent manuscripts, and maps Emaré and its adjacent works.

evidence.citation: Anne Laskaya and Eve Salisbury, eds., introduction and edition of Emaré, The Middle English Breton Lays (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1995; revised METS online edition).
evidence.locator: introduction; manuscript and critical-edition bibliography; text and notes
evidence.summary: Identifies the unique Cotton Caligula A II witness, dates its dialect to the late fourteenth century and Northeast Midlands or East Anglia, treats Emaré as a Middle English Breton lay, and discusses the poem's form, narrative, cloth, analogues, and lack of a directly known source.

evidence.citation: Joseph Ritson, ed., Ancient Engleish Metrical Romanceës, vol. 2 (London: W. Bulmer and Company for G. and W. Nicol, 1802), 204-247.
evidence.summary: Contains the first printed edition of Emare; Gough calls it the only prior edition in 1901, and Rickert and DIME place it first in their edition histories.

evidence.citation: Denise C. White, 'BL Cotton Caligula Aii, Manuscript Context, The Theme of Obedience, and a Diplomatic Transcription Edition' (PhD diss., Georgia State University, 2012), doi:10.57709/3230402.
evidence.locator: Emaré discussion pp. 123-155; diplomatic transcription pp. 326-337
evidence.summary: Provides a later codex-context study and diplomatic transcription visibly mapping Emaré from f. 71ra through f. 76vb.

open_questions.question: Can Emaré's composition date and original dialect or place be established more narrowly than late fourteenth century and Northeast Midlands or East Anglia?
next_steps: Review current linguistic work that distinguishes authorial dialect from the South-East or South-East Midlands language of the unique fifteenth-century copy.
Preserve competing localizations as attributed claims rather than converting dialect evidence into an exact place of composition.

open_questions.question: What source or source tradition immediately underlies the Middle English Emaré?
next_steps: Review post-Rickert comparative work on the Constance or accused-queen traditions and the Breton-lay claim in lines 1030-1032.
Do not create a specific lost French-source entity from Rickert's linguistic and analogical reconstruction unless later transmission evidence supports it.

notes: Current foliation places Emaré on ff. 71r-76v; Rickert and Gough record legacy foliation ff. 69-74. These are two locator systems for one physical witness.
The work's language, composition date, and possible dialect region are not inherited from Rickert's Chicago imprint or the unique witness's modern holding location.
The complete-source facsimile question and the separate rights for manuscript photographs, editions, encoding, and repository artifact are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
